title-group.article-title: Inactivation of Ink4a/Arf leads to deregulated expression of miRNAs in K-Ras transgenic mouse model of pancreatic cancer

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Oncology, Karmanos Cancer Institute, Wayne State University, Detroit, Michigan
2Department of Pathology, Karmanos Cancer Institute, Wayne State University, Detroit, Michigan
3Department of Medicine, Indiana University, Indianapolis, Indiana

author-notes.corresp: *Address for Correspondence: Fazlul H. Sarkar Ph.D., Department of Pathology, Karmanos Cancer Institute, Wayne State University School of Medicine, 740 Hudson Webber Cancer Research Center, 4100 John R Street, Detroit, MI 48201, Tel: 313-576-8327, Fax: 313-576-8389, fsarkar@med.wayne.edu

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

abstract.p: Human Pancreatic Cancer (PC) is an aggressive disease, which has been recapitulated in transgenic animal model that provides unique opportunity for mechanistic understanding of disease progression and also for testing the efficacy of novel therapeutics. Emerging evidence suggests deregulated expression of microRNAs (miRNAs) in human PC, and thus we investigated the expression of miRNAs in pancreas tissues obtained from transgenic mouse models of K-Ras (K), Pdx1-Cre (C), K-Ras; Pdx1-Cre (KC) and K-Ras; Pdx1-Cre; INK4a/Arf (KCI), initially from pooled RNA samples using miRNA profiling, and further confirmed in individual specimens by quantitative RT-PCR. We found over-expression of miR-21, miR-221, miR-27a, miR-27b and miR-155, and down-regulation of miR-216a, miR-216b, miR-217 and miR-146a expression in tumors derived from KC and KCI mouse model, which was consistent with data from KCI-derived RInk-1 cells. Mechanistic investigations revealed a significant induction of EGFR, K-Ras, and MT1-MMP protein expression in tissues from both KC and KCI mouse compared to tissues from K or C, and these results were consistent with similar findings in RInk-1 cells compared to human MIAPaCa-2 cells. Furthermore, miR-155 knock-down in RInk-1 cells resulted in the inhibition of cell growth and colony formation consistent with down-regulation of EGFR, MT1-MMP and K-Ras expression. In addition, miR-216b which target Ras, and forced re-expression of miR-216b in RInk-1 cells showed inhibition of cell proliferation and colony formation, which was correlated with reduced expression of Ras, EGFR and MT1-MMP. These findings suggest that these models would be useful for preclinical evaluation of novel miRNA-targeted agents for designing personalized therapy for PC.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Pancreatic cancer (PC) is the fourth leading cause of cancer related deaths in the United States (Siegel et al, 2011) and it is virtually fatal to all patients diagnosed with PC. Diagnosis of PC at an early stage is a key factor in determining improved survival. The major difficulty in treating PC is also because of its overt and early metastatic potential, late diagnosis, and profound resistance to conventional therapeutics. In order to gain mechanistic insight and also for testing the efficacy of novel therapeutics, many researchers have adopted transgenic animal models especially K-Ras derived models among others.

Ras is a small GTPase that plays a central role in protein kinase signaling pathways, and regulates cell proliferation and apoptosis (Pylayeva-Gupta et al, 2011). K-Ras mutations are frequently found in PC, and occurs during early stages of tumor initiation (Kim et al, 2011; Krantz et al, 2011; Mohri et al, 2011; Ray et al, 2011) which is believed to arise through the progression of low grade to high-grade pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia (Pan1N) (Hruban et al, 2006). Identifying these PC precursors and associated biomarkers, has the potential for diagnostic accuracy which will aid in the early detection of these lesions in individuals at high-risk for PC (Cheung et al, 2008). Hence several mouse models have been created to mimic human PC that has been proven useful for studying tumor progression, prevention and therapy (Aguirre et al, 2003; Cook et al, 2008; Frese and Tuveson, 2007; Hingorani et al, 2003; Hingorani et al, 2005; Maniati et al, 2011; Olive et al, 2009; Tuveson and Hingorani, 2005; Wang et al, 2011). One such model with K-Ras mutation in combination with Ink4a/Arf deficiency resulted in the activation of Notch and NF-κB signaling as reported in our recent publication (Wang et al, 2011) showing tumor progression, which could in part be also due to deregulated expression of microRNAs (miRNAs) and their target genes.

Experimental evidence supports an increasing role for miRNAs in the molecular biology of PC. These small molecules are known to play significant roles in animals and humans at the post-transcriptional level and act as either oncogenes or tumor suppressors (Ali et al, 2010b; Bao et al, 2011). The deregulated expression of miRNAs and their target genes in tumor cells or in the plasma as documented in our previous studies (Ali et al, 2010a; Ali et al, 2010b; Bao et al, 2011; Li et al, 2010; Wang et al, 2010) could become useful for early diagnosis and for assessing therapeutic outcome in PC. Previous studies have shown up-regulation of epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) in many malignancies including PC (Gadgeel et al, 2009; Li et al, 2010; Pryczynicz et al, 2008), and its expression was found to be associated with large tumor size, decrease survival, advanced clinical stage (Li et al, 2010), and was also associated with lower expression of miR-146a (Edmonds et al, 2009; Kogo et al, 2011; Li et al, 2010), which provided strong evidence in support of the deregulated expression of miRNA and EGFR in PC. The blockade of EGFR pathway by gefitinib as a chemo-preventive agent in K-Ras transgenic mouse model suppressed tumor cell proliferation, by inhibiting Pan1N formation (Mohammed et al, 2010). Interestingly, membrane type 1-matrix metalloproteinase (MT1-MMP) has been found to be associated with metastasis and its over-expression in PC contributed to gemcitabine resistance both in vitro and in vivo (ngi-Garimella et al, 2011; Ottaviano et al, 2006). The over-expression of MT1-MMP was also found to be associated with decreased expression of let-7 miRNA in human pancreatic tumor specimens (ngi-Garimella et al, 2011). Moreover, increased Snail expression, a known marker for epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) driven by increased TGF-β1 signaling, promotes invasion of PC cells, which was found to be associated with increased expression of MT1-MMP (Shields et al, 2011), suggesting that re-expression of let-7 could inactivate MT1-MMP and thereby reduce the potential of invasive characteristics of PC cells.

Recent studies have confirmed that miRNAs are present in remarkably stable form in cells and in tumors, and thus it can serve as biomarkers to distinguish cancer from non-cancer (Nakata et al, 2011; Zhao et al, 2011). A recent report from our laboratory showed a significant difference in the expression levels of several miRNAs in the plasma of PC patients compared to normal subjects (Ali et al, 2010b), suggesting that the assessment for the levels of deregulated expression of miRNAs and their targets either in the tumor specimens or in the plasma could be linked with the biology of the development and progression of PC. Our previous studies have shown that the activated K-ras and Ink4a/Arf deficiency cooperate during the development of PC in the transgenic animal models by activation of Notch and NF-κB signaling pathways (Wang et al, 2011). Therefore, the goal of this study was to assess differential expression of miRNAs in pooled pancreatic tissue samples (n=4) from K-RasG12D (K) and Pdx1-Cre (C), compared to pancreatic tumor tissues of K-RasG12D; Pdx1-Cre (KC) and K-RasG12D; Pdx1-Cre; Ink4a/Arf (KCI) mouse models.

We found differential expression of miRNA that could distinguish the four different groups, and thus we focused further studies using selected miRNAs whose expression was either lost or up-regulated in the tumors. The expression of the top 9 up- and down-regulated individual miRNAs was independently quantitated in individual samples by qRT-PCR, and their expression was further investigated in the KCI-derived RInk-1 cells. We also investigated the protein expression of EGFR, K-Ras and MT1-MMP in the animal tissues and in the RInk-1 cells. We found that miR-216b and miR-155 possess a match for base pairing with the 3′-UTR of K-Ras with context score percentile of 79 and 77, respectively as assessed by miRNA target prediction algorithms (TargetScanHuman 5.2). Recent studies have shown that oncogenic Ras up-regulates matrix metalloproteinase expression through Mitogen-activated protein kinase (MAPK) and Jun-kinase (JNK) (Kranenburg et al, 2004; Pylayeva-Gupta et al, 2011), which in turn could be responsible for deregulated expression of certain miRNAs. Therefore, we chose to conduct further mechanistic studies by investigating the consequence of transfecting pre-miR-216b, and anti-sense oligonucleotide (ASO) for miR-155 in RInk-1 cells. Here we report, for the first time, that transfection of pre-miR-216b and ASO-miR-155 suppressed cell proliferation and clonogenicity, which was correlated with reduced expression of not only K-Ras but also EGFR and MT1-MMP.

sec.title: Materials and Methods
Ethics Statement
sec.p: This study was conducted in strict accordance with the recommendations in the guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals of the National Institutes of health. The protocol was approved by the Committee on the Ethics of animal experiments of Wayne State University.

sec.title: Mouse Model
sec.p: The LSL-K-RasG12D strain was bred to the following strains: LSL-K-RasG12D; Pdx1-Cre, and LSL-K-RasG12D; Pdx1-Cre; INK4a/Arf lox/lox as previously described (Carriere et al, 2009; Seeley et al, 2009). Pancreata were collected from LSL-K-RasG12D (K), Pdx1-Cre (C), LSL-K-RasG12D; Pdx1-Cre (KC), LSL-K-RasG12D Pdx1-Cre; INK4a/Arf (KCI) mouse models and were harvested for both protein and RNA extraction and used for further studies.

sec.title: Genotyping
sec.p: Genomic DNA was extracted from tail cuttings using the REDExtract-N-Amp Tissue PCR kit (Sigma St. Louis, MO). PCR reactions were repeated three times to confirm the presence of the oncogenic K-Ras, and Pdx1-Cre transgenes.

sec.title: Cells Culture
sec.p: Human and mouse pancreatic cancer cell lines MIAPaCa-2, and RInk-1 were chosen for this study. RInk-1 murine pancreatic tumor cell line was generated from the LSL-K-RasG12D; Pdx1-Cre; INK4a/Arf (KCI) mice as described previously (Seeley et al, 2009). MIAPaCa-2 cell line has been tested and authenticated in the core facility “Applied Genomics Technology Center” at Wayne State University on March 13, 2009. The method used for testing was short tandem repeat (STR) profiling using the PowerPlex® 16 System from Promega (Madison, WI). The cells were stored in liquid nitrogen in multiple vials.

sec.title: RNA Isolation
sec.p: The RNA was isolated using mirVana miRNA isolation kit (Ambion, Inc.) according to the manufacturer’s protocol. Briefly, the tissue/cells were homogenized with a douncer in lysis solution and incubated with 1/10 volume of miRNA homogenate additive for 10 minutes. Equal volume of Acid-Phenol:Chloroform was added to the mixture and vortexed for a minute before centrifugation to separate aqueous phase. About 1.25 volume of 100% ethanol was added to the aqueous phase before being applied directly to the filter cartridge. The RNA was washed with the buffers provided with the kit to remove impurities and eluted in a final volume of 100 μl.

sec.title: MicroRNA (miRNA) profiling
sec.p: The pooled purified RNA samples of pancreatic tissues from K, C, KC and KCI mice were analyzed by LC Sciences for miRNA microarray profiling (LC Sciences Houston, TX). Network analysis was performed with the Ingenuity pathway Analysis (IPA) software (Ingenuity Systems). Ingenuity Pathway Analysis is a web-based bioinformatics tool to identify pathways and functional networks.

sec.title: TaqMan miRNA Real-Time quantitative Reverse Transcriptase-Polymerase Chain Reaction (qRT-PCR)
sec.p: Quantitative RT-PCR is the standard method for validation of microarray profiling data which provides quantitative analysis of miRNA expression in real time for the miRNAs of interest from the samples analyzed by microarray profiling. Hence the expression of five oncogenes and 4 tumor suppressor miRNAs of all four groups K, C, KC and KCI were determined using TaqMan MicroRNA Assay kit (Applied Biosystems) following manufacturer’s protocol. About 10 ng of RNA from each sample was reverse transcribed as described earlier (Ali et al, 2010b) and RT-PCR reactions were then carried out in a total volume of 10 μl reaction mixture as described earlier (Ali et al, 2010b). All reactions, including controls were performed in triplicate using StepOnePlus Real-Time PCR (Applied Biosystems). Relative expression of miRNAs was analyzed using Ct method and was normalized by RNU6B expression.

sec.title: Protein extraction and Western blot analysis
sec.p: The total protein was extracted from all four groups K, C, KC and KCI of transgenic mice, and also from MIAPaCa-2, and RInk-1 cell lines and subjected to western blot analysis as described previously (Ali et al, 2010b) to evaluate the expression status of EGFR, K-Ras and MT1-MMP.

sec.title: Anti-sense miR-155 and Pre-miR-216b oligonucleotide Transfection
sec.p: RInk-1 cells were plated in 6 well plates overnight. Cells were transfected with either anti-sense miR-155 oligonucleotide (ASO), or Pre-miR-216b, or control miRNA (Ambion, Austin, TX) at a final concentration of 20nM using DharmaFECT transfection reagent (Dharmacon), following the manufacturer protocol. After 48–72h of transfection, the transfected cells were measured for cell growth by MTT and clonogenic assay, and also harvested for the extraction of total RNA and protein using standard methods.

sec.title: Cell Viability Assay of transfected cells
sec.p: Standard assay using 3-(4,5-dimetylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was performed after transfection for 72 h, and experiments were repeated three times. The color intensity was measured by TECAN’s microplate fluorometer (TECAN, Research Triangle Park, NC) at 595 nm.

sec.title: Clonogenic assay of transfected cells
sec.p: Transfected cells were trypsinized and 1000 viable cells were plated in 100 mm petri dishes. The cells were then incubated for about 10–12 days at 37°C in a 5% CO2/5% O2/90% N2 incubator. Colonies were stained with 2% crystal violet and quantitated.

sec.title: Statistical Methods
sec.p: Comparisons of treatment outcome were tested for statistical difference by the paired t- test. Statistical significance was assumed at a p value of < 0.05.

sec.title: Results
Expression profiling of miRNA from pancreatic tissue samples characterized 187 miRNAs that were differentially expressed between LSL-K-RasG12D; Pdx1-Cre; Ink4a (KCI) and LSL-K-RasG12D; Pdx1-Cre (KC)
sec.p: For the current study, 5 μl of RNA from 4 animal’s pancreatic tissue specimens of each group K, C, KC and KCI were pooled separately and subjected to miRNA profiling. The miRNA expression profiling revealed 187 miRNAs that were differentially expressed, of which 109 were up-regulated and 78 were down-regulated, when compared to either K or C. We observed up-and down-regulation of several miRNAs, including miR-21, miR-221, miR-27a, miR-27b, miR-155, miR-216a, miR-216b, miR-217 and miR-146a in KCI compared to KC animals. In order to understand the pathway and target genes of the deregulated miRNAs from KCI and KC groups compared to either K or C, we performed Ingenuity modeling of the miRNA profiling as presented in the following section.

sec.title: Pathway analysis of miRNA expression level changes between KCI and KC animals compared with K or C animals
sec.p: The underlying mechanism on the differences in the expression levels between the KCI and KC compared to either K or C was studied by Ingenuity pathway analysis as presented in Figure 1A. The data set of differentially expressed miRNAs in KCI, KC, K or C was uploaded into the IPA software. Network of these miRNAs were then algorithmically generated based on their connectivity. This network also showed that many of the miRNAs are regulated by each other either directly or indirectly, and are also regulated by several target genes or vice versa. We found up-regulation (red) and down-regulation (green) of several miRNAs. We also observed 16 bio-functional network groups which included cancer, inflammatory disease, and gastrointestinal disease (Figure 1B) as demonstrated through the Ingenuity pathway analysis. Of the nine miRNAs chosen for further analysis based on the miRNA profiling data, five were oncogenic (miR-155, miR-21, miR-221, miR-27a, and miR-27b) and four functions as tumor suppressors (miR-216a, miR-216b, miR-217, and miR-146a). We further validated these nine miRNAs in KCI, KC, K and C individually with real-time PCR. The data is presented in the subsequent sections.

sec.title: Up-regulation of five oncogenic miRNAs in LSL-K-RasG12D; Pdx1-Cre; INK4a/Arf (KCI) tumors as assessed by qRT-PCR
sec.p: The miRNA expression analysis by qRT-PCR was blinded to the source of the samples. The samples from all four groups; K, C, KC and KCI were tested individually in parallel to avoid batch-effects differences. We confirmed the findings of five oncogenic miRNAs; miR-21, miR-221, miR-27a, miR-27b and miR-155 with real-time qRT-PCR, showing a significant increase in KCI group compared to the other three groups; K, C and KC as shown in Figure 2.

sec.title: Down-regulation of four tumor suppressor miRNAs in LSL-K-RasG12D; Pdx1- Cre; INK4a/Arf (KCI) tumors as assessed by qRT-PCR
sec.p: The miRNA expression analysis of the four functional tumor suppressor miRNAs such as miR-216a, miR-216b, miR-217 and miR-146a (Figure 3), were performed individually in all samples by qRT-PCR which showed significant reduction in the expression levels in KC and KCI samples compared to either K or C samples. The tissue-based findings from these animal tissue specimens were further tested in human pancreas cancer cells and also the cells derived from the KCI mouse as presented below.

sec.title: Differential expression of miRNAs in MIAPaCa-2 and RInk-1 cell lines
sec.p: We compared the basal levels of expression of miRNAs (the same five oncogenic and four tumor suppressor miRNAs) in human pancreatic cancer cell line MIAPaCa-2 and mouse RInk-1 cells derived from the KCI mouse. We found that all five oncogenic miRNAs were significantly up-regulated in RInk-1 cells compared to MIAPaCa-2 cell line (Figure 4). On the contrary, none of the tumor suppressor miRNAs which showed expression level in all four tumor groups remained undetermined in both RInk-1 and MIAPaCa-2 cells (due to complete loss of expression), supporting highly aggressive nature of these cells. The CT values above 36 were considered undetermined.

sec.title: Elevated levels of expression of EGFR, K-Ras and MT1-MMP proteins in LSL-K-RasG12D; Pdx1-Cre; INK4a/Arf (KCI), and K-Ras; Pdx1-Cre (KC) tissues as assessed by western blot analysis
sec.p: We examined the basal levels of expression of EGFR, K-Ras and MT1-MMP proteins in pooled samples of all four groups; K, C, KC and KCI transgenic animal tissues. We found that the protein expression of EGFR, K-Ras and MT1-MMP were all highly elevated in the pancreatic tumors of KC and KCI animals compared to K or C animals (Figure 5A). Similarly, we also compared the protein expression in the K-Ras mutant MIAPaCa-2 cells compared to murine RInk-1 cells derived from the KCI animals. We found up-regulated expression of EGFR in RInk-1 cells compared to MIAPaCa-2 cells whereas MT1-MMP expression was higher in MIAPaCa-2 cells compared to Rink-1 cells. The level of expression of K-Ras was found to be very high in both the cell lines (Figure 5B).

sec.title: Transfection of anti-sense miR-155 in RInk-1 cells reduced cell growth, colony formation, and protein expression
sec.p: Since miR-155 was found to be significantly up-regulated in KCI tumors, and also in RInk-1 cells (derived from KCI animal), we tested whether the inactivation of miR-155 using anti-sense oligonucleotide ASO-miR-155 transfection could have any effects on cell proliferation and clonogenic growth. The cells were seeded in both 96 and 6 well plates and were transfected with ASO-miR-155 for 48 to 72h. Inactivation of miR-155 significantly inhibited cell viability and clonogenicity in the ASO-miR-155 transfected cells compared to control miRNA transfected cells (Figure 6A), which was correlated with decreased expression of miR-155 as assessed by qRT-PCR (Figure 6A). Interestingly, inactivation of miR-155 led to decreased expression of EGFR, K-Ras and MT1-MMP in cells transfected with ASO-miR-155.

sec.title: Transfection of pre-miR-216b precursor in RInk-1 cells reduced cell growth, colony formation, and protein expression
sec.p: Since the expression of miR-216b was found to be significantly down-regulated or undetermined in RInk-1 cells, we transfected these cells with pre-miR-216b precursor in both 96 and 6 well plated for 48 to 72h. Re-expression of miR-216b led to a significant decrease in cell viability and clonogenicity compared to control miRNA transfected cells (Figure 6B). Re-expression of miR-216b with pre-miR-216b was found to be consistent with increased miR-216b expression, which was further correlated with decreased expression of EGFR, K-Ras and MT1-MMP (Figure 6B).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Pancreatic Cancer (PC) is a lethal disease for which novel therapeutics are urgently needed especially because conventional therapies have had limited success due to intrinsic and extrinsic resistance to cytotoxic agents (Sarkar et al, 2009; Wang et al, 2010; Xiong et al, 2006). Since proto-oncogene K-Ras mutations are the most common variation amongst many human cancers including PC (Smit et al, 1988), attempts has been made to target K-Ras with inhibitors like farnesyl transferase, which has shown to be completely ineffective (Macdonald et al, 2005). Several mouse models have been created to mimic human PC that has been proven useful for studying tumor progression, prevention and therapy (Aguirre et al, 2003; Cook et al, 2008; Frese and Tuveson, 2007; Hingorani et al, 2003; Hingorani et al, 2005; Maniati et al, 2011; Olive et al, 2009; Tuveson and Hingorani, 2005; Wang et al, 2011). One such model with K-Ras mutation in combination with Ink4a/Arf deficiency resulted in the activation of Notch and NF-κB signaling as reported in our recent publication (Wang et al, 2011) showing tumor progression, which could in part be also due to deregulated expression of microRNAs (miRNAs) and their target genes.

In order to further understand the likely mechanism(s) involved in the progression of PC in the LSL-K-RasG12D; Pdx1-Cre; INK4a/Arf (KCI), and LSL-K-RasG12D; Pdx1-Cre (KC) mouse models, we analyzed a number of miRNAs by miRNA profiling compared to pancreata of the K-RasG12D (K), and Pdx1-Cre (C) mouse. Expression profiles of miRNAs could be highly informative in discriminating malignant from the normal pancreas because of the unique patterns of expression of miRNAs in tumors. We found 187 miRNAs that were differentially expressed in the KCI and KC tumors compared to K or C tissue specimens. A systems biology approach is a distinct approach for investigating networks of molecular measures that drive assorted signaling pathways resulting from the deregulations of miRNAs as demonstrated in figure 1. The knowledge gained from such pathway analysis provided the scientific rationale for choosing specific miRNAs for mechanistic studies as discussed later.

A recent report has shown that miR-21 is up-regulated by K-Ras and EGFR in PC cell lines (du Rieu et al, 2010), and here we report for the first time our results demonstrating higher expression of K-Ras, EGFR and MT1-MMP in the tumors of KCI mouse and these results were associated with up-regulated expression of several miRNAs including miR-21, miR-221, miR-155, miR-27a, b in KCI and KC mouse tumors. Moreover, we found down-regulated expression of miR-216a, b, and miR-217 in KCI and KC mouse tumors compared to the pancreata of K or C mouse. The role of EGFR over-expression has been well established in many human cancers (Gadgeel et al, 2007; Gadgeel et al, 2009; Mohammed et al, 2010; Pryczynicz et al, 2008), and transgenic mouse model (Ohashi et al, 2009). EGFR promotes miR-21 production, a key oncogenic miRNA in the multi-step progression of PC precursor lesions (du Rieu et al, 2010), and is also linked with the activation of the AP-1 transcription factor in response to Ras (Talotta et al, 2009). The increased levels of miR-21 in the plasma of PC patients has been found to correlate with worst survival as documented in our recent publication (Ali et al, 2010b), suggesting that over-expression of miR-21 found in the tumors of KCI and KC mouse is highly relevant to human PC and as such miR-21 targeted agents could become a newer therapeutic approach for the treatment of human PC. To that end, we have recently reported the therapeutic effects of our novel synthetic analog of curcumin, CDF in preclinical in vitro and in vivo studies documenting that CDF could inactivate the expression of miR-21 (Bao et al, 2011). In a recent study, miR-217 was found to be down-regulated in PC both in vitro and in vivo and was further associated with Ras (Zhao et al, 2010). Our current findings clearly show significant down-regulation in the expression of miR-217 in the tumors derived from KCI and KC mouse, which is consistent with the above published report.

We also found deregulated expression of miR-155, which is consistent with findings reported in chronic myeloid leukemia where the expression of miR-155 was linked with the expression of K-Ras and EGFR (Machova et al, 2011). Other studies evaluated the role of miR-155 in normal tissues, premalignant lesions and invasive carcinoma in the mouse model showing an increased expression of miR-155 during the progression of normal tissues to invasive PC, which was further confirmed in tissues and blood samples of PC patients (LaConti et al, 2011). These results are consistent with our findings showing that over-expression of miR-155 is associated with increased expression of K-Ras and EGFR, which could indeed be responsible for tumor aggressiveness. Our data clearly show that inactivation of miR-155 in RInk-1 cells by ASO-miR-155 transfection could down-regulate the expression of EGFR and K-Ras, which was correlated with inhibition of cell growth and clonogenicity. However, in addition to being expressed in cancer cells, miR-155 is also expressed in a variety of immune-modulating cells, including macrophages, leukocytes and T cells, and its expression is known to be increased by pro-inflammatory cytokines (O’Connell et al, 2007; Rodriguez et al, 2007). These observations raise the possibility that in KCI mice the pancreatic tumors express high levels of miR-155 due to the abundance of cancer cells and inflammatory cells within the tumor microenvironment in this model.

Another important key player in tumor progression is membrane type 1-matrix metalloproteinase (MT1-MMP) that stimulate cell migration and proliferation (D’Alessio et al, 2008). According to a recent study, down-regulation of MT1-MMP in orthotopic model of breast cancer significantly inhibited lung metastasis, but not tumor growth (Perentes et al, 2011). The expression of MT1-MMP is also considered as an independent predictive marker of overall survival in women with breast cancer (McGowan and Duffy, 2008). Here, we report for the first time, showing increased expression of MT1-MMP in both KC and KCI transgenic mouse models whose expression could be attenuated in cells transfected with pre-miR-216a, resulting in the inhibition of cell growth and clonogenicity. These results suggest the biological implication of targeted re-expression of miR-216a or inactivation of miR-155 by novel approach could become newer therapy for the treatment of human PC in the future. In conclusion, we have provided experimental evidence, using the KC and KCI mouse models as well as by using the RInk-1 cells derived from the KCI mouse, supporting that targeting miRNAs could be a novel approach for therapy. Therefore, it is tempting to speculate that inactivation of Ras, EGFR and MT1-MMP by targeted re-expression of miRNAs or down-regulation of miRNAs would be useful for the treatment of PC.

ack.p: This work was funded by grants from the National Cancer Institute, NIH 5R01CA131151, 3R01CA131151-02S109, and 1R01CA132794 awarded to FHS. We also thank Puschelberg and Guido foundations for their generous contribution.

element-citation.article-title: Activated Kras and Ink4a/Arf deficiency cooperate to produce metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma
Gemcitabine sensitivity can be induced in pancreatic cancer cells through modulation of miR-200 and miR-21 expression by curcumin or its analogue CDF 4
Differentially expressed miRNAs in the plasma may provide a molecular signature for aggressive pancreatic cancer 3
Anti-Tumor Activity of a Novel Compound-CDF Is Mediated by Regulating miR-21, miR-200, and PTEN in Pancreatic Cancer 2

Application of a global proteomic approach to archival precursor lesions: deleted in malignant brain tumors 1 and tissue transglutaminase 2 are upregulated in pancreatic cancer precursors

Tissue inhibitor of metalloproteinases-2 binding to membrane-type 1 matrix metalloproteinase induces MAPK activation and cell growth by a non-proteolytic mechanism
MicroRNA-21 is induced early in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma precursor lesions
Breast cancer metastasis suppressor 1 coordinately regulates metastasis-associated microRNA expression
Maximizing mouse cancer models
Response to dual blockade of epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) and cycloxygenase-2 in nonsmall cell lung cancer may be dependent on the EGFR mutational status of the tumor
Genistein enhances the effect of epidermal growth factor receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitors and inhibits nuclear factor kappa B in nonsmall cell lung cancer cell lines
Preinvasive and invasive ductal pancreatic cancer and its early detection in the mouse
Trp53R172H and KrasG12D cooperate to promote chromosomal instability and widely metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma in mice

Clinical significance of miR-146a in gastric cancer cases
Stimulation of angiogenesis by Ras proteins
MT1-MMP cooperates with Kras(G12D) to promote pancreatic fibrosis through increased TGF-beta signaling
Tissue and serum microRNAs in the Kras(G12D) transgenic animal model and in patients with pancreatic cancer
miR-146a suppresses invasion of pancreatic cancer cells
A phase II study of farnesyl transferase inhibitor R115777 in pancreatic cancer: a Southwest oncology group (SWOG 9924) study
Expression patterns of microRNAs associated with CML phases and their disease related targets
Crosstalk between the canonical NF-kappaB and Notch signaling pathways inhibits Ppargamma expression and promotes pancreatic cancer progression in mice
Matrix metalloproteinase expression and outcome in patients with breast cancer: analysis of a published database
The epidermal growth factor receptor inhibitor gefitinib prevents the progression of pancreatic lesions to carcinoma in a conditional LSL-KrasG12D/+ transgenic mouse model

element-citation.source: Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A

element-citation.article-title: Chemopreventive effects of gefitinib on nonsmoking-related lung tumorigenesis in activating epidermal growth factor receptor transgenic mice
Inhibition of Hedgehog signaling enhances delivery of chemotherapy in a mouse model of pancreatic cancer
Extracellular matrix-mediated membrane-type 1 matrix metalloproteinase expression in pancreatic ductal cells is regulated by transforming growth factor-beta1
Cancer cell-associated MT1-MMP promotes blood vessel invasion and distant metastasis in triple-negative mammary tumors
Expression of EGF and EGFR strongly correlates with metastasis of pancreatic ductal carcinoma
RAS oncogenes: weaving a tumorigenic web

Requirement of bic/microRNA-155 for normal immune function
Pancreatic cancer stem cells and EMT in drug resistance and metastasis 73
Pancreatic cancer and precursor pancreatic intraepithelial neoplasia lesions are devoid of primary cilia
Pancreatic cancer cells respond to type I collagen by inducing snail expression to promote membrane type 1 matrix metalloproteinase-dependent collagen invasion
Cancer statistics, 2011: the impact of eliminating socioeconomic and racial disparities on premature cancer deaths
KRAS codon 12 mutations occur very frequently in pancreatic adenocarcinomas
An autoregulatory loop mediated by miR-21 and PDCD4 controls the AP-1 activity in RAS transformation 41
Ductal pancreatic cancer in humans and mice
element-citation.source: Cold Spring Harb Symp Quant Biol

element-citation.article-title: Activated K-ras and INK4a/Arf deficiency cooperate during the development of pancreatic cancer by activation of Notch and NF-kappaB signaling pathways
Targeting miRNAs involved in cancer stem cell and EMT regulation: An emerging concept in overcoming drug resistance
Cytotoxic chemotherapy for pancreatic cancer: Advances to date and future directions
The miR-217 microRNA functions as a potential tumor suppressor in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma by targeting KRAS
Genomic Profiling of MicroRNAs and Proteomics Reveals an Early Molecular Alteration Associated with Tumorigenesis Induced by MC-LR in Mice

caption.p: Ingenuity network analysis showing up (red) and down-regulation (green) of miRNAs as analyzed by miRNA profiling that are involved in pancreatic cancer (PC) and their target genes (A). The solid lines connecting genes represent a direct relation and dotted lines represent indirect relationship. We also observed 16 bio-functional network groups that included cancer, genetic disorder, and gastrointestinal disease (B).

Comparative level of expression of miR-21, miR-221, miR-27a, miR-27b, and miR-155 in the pancreata of K-Ras (K), Pdx1-Cre (C), K-Ras; Pdx1-Cre; (KC), and K-Ras; Pdx1-Cre; INK4a/Arf (KCI) mouse. There was a significant up-regulation in the expression of miR-21, miR-221, miR-27a,b and miR-155 in KCI tumors compared to either K or C or KC pancreata. P values represent comparison between K and KCI as calculated by the paired t test.

Comparative level of expression of miR-216a, miR-216b, miR-217 and miR-146 in the pancreata of K-Ras (K), Pdx1-Cre (C), K-Ras; Pdx1-Cre; (KC), and K-Ras; Pdx1-Cre; INK4a/Arf (KCI) mouse. There was a significant down-regulation in the expression of miR-216a, miR-216b, miR-217 and miR-146a in KCI tumors compared to either K or C or KC pancreata. P values represent comparison between K, and KCI as calculated by the paired t test.

Comparative levels of expression of miR-21, miR-221, miR-27a, miR-27b and miR-155 in MIAPaCa-2 and RInk-1 cells. There was a significant up-regulation in the expression of miR-21, miR-221, miR-27a, miR-27b, and miR-155 in murine RInk-1 cells compared to human MIAPaCa-2 PC cells. P values represent comparison between MIAPaCa-2 and RInk-1 cells as calculated by the paired t test.

Basal level of expression of EGFR, K-Ras and MT1-MMP in the pancreata of K, C, KC and KCI (A), and MIAPaCa-2 and RInk-1 cells as assessed by western blot analysis. A significant over-expression of EGFR, K-Ras and MT1-MMP was found in KC and KCI tumors compared to either K or C pancreata. Similarly, in RInk-1 cells EGFR was over-expressed whereas the expression of MT1-MMP was lower in RInk-1 cells compared to MIAPaCa-2 cells.

Inactivation of miR-155 expression by ASO-miR-155 transfection (A), and re-expression of miR-216b by pre-miR-216b precursor transfection (B) led to reduced cell viability as assessed by MTT assay, and clonogenicity as determined by colony formation assay, which was associated with decreased levels of EGFR, K-Ras and MT1-MMP expression as assessed by western blot, consistent with decreased and increased expression of miR-155 and miR-216b, respectively as assessed by qRT-PCR.
